Significantly more TPP teachers encouraged higher-order thinking, had students cite textual evidence in writing, and engaged students in reading, writing, and speaking about texts, especially in the second year of implementation.

entries.title: Teacher Factors That Drive Improvement in Secondary Writing Outcomes: A Landscape Analysis
entries.desc: This Gates Foundation-funded brief synthesizes an academic literature review identifying instructional skills and practices that support writing improvements for grades 6–12 students. Teachers influence writing outcomes both through explicit instructional practices and through subtler means — how they engage with students, provide support, and offer feedback — making teacher development a critical lever for secondary writing improvement.

entries.title: What Matters for Student Achievement? Exploring Teacher Instructional Practices and the Role of School-Level and Student-Level Characteristics
entries.desc: This exploratory study analyzed data from 10,716 students taught by 214 ELA teachers across 63 schools to identify which instructional practices relate to higher student achievement. General instruction and classroom management practices — fostering engagement, facilitating discussion, minimizing disruptions, and maintaining a positive climate — showed significant associations with achievement, more so than specific reading/writing strategies. School characteristics like racial diversity, student-teacher ratios, and enrollment moderated these relationships.

entries.title: Strengthening Principal Preparation Through On-The-Job Training: Insights from KIPP's Strategies for Developing Assistant Principals as School Leaders
entries.desc: This publication examines how KIPP, the nation's largest charter school network, developed a structured internal program to cultivate school leaders by turning the assistant principal experience into a deliberate leadership development pathway. Rather than relying solely on external recruitment, KIPP's strategies address the ongoing need for qualified principals through on-the-job training and competency-building for assistant principals.

entries.title: Implementation and Impacts of Pay-for-Performance: The 2010 Teacher Incentive Fund (TIF) Grantees After Three Years
entries.desc: This NCEE study snapshot examines how districts implemented performance-based compensation systems funded through 2010 federal Teacher Incentive Fund grants. After three years, pay-for-performance had small positive impacts on students' math and reading achievement in the ten evaluated districts. However, few districts structured bonuses to align well with TIF guidance, and many teachers misunderstood their eligibility or potential earnings.

entries.title: Districts and Data: Developing Capacity for Effective Data Use
entries.desc: This issue brief examines the challenges school districts face in using data strategically, noting that while large interconnected datasets have raised educator awareness of data's value, many districts struggle to identify the right questions, datasets, or interpretive methods. The brief offers four recommendations: build a culture of data use, ask the right questions, correctly interpret findings, and dedicate adequate resources to analysis.

entries.title: How Community Colleges Address Basic Needs and Financial Stability of Low-Income Students to Boost College Completion
entries.desc: This report examines the Working Students Success Network (WSSN), a comprehensive support strategy for low-income students and students of color across 19 community colleges, integrating financial literacy, budgeting, and career planning with credential attainment. The research found that receiving intensive one-on-one service mattered more than multi-pillar group services, though colleges could reach more students through group-based delivery when individualized approaches were adapted to individual needs.

entries.title: From Outreach to Enrollment: Strategies to Engage Adults in Education Beyond High School
entries.desc: This Lumina Foundation-funded brief examines marketing, outreach, and enrollment strategies employed by states receiving Adult Promise grants to engage adult learners (ages 25–64) in higher education. Key findings emphasize that appropriate messaging is critical because adult learners' priorities differ from traditional enrollees, high-touch personalized guidance through enrollment and financial aid is essential, and digital tools can make the enrollment process more convenient for adults balancing work and family demands.

entries.title: Infusing Academics into Career and Technical Education
entries.desc: This issue brief examines the integration of academic content — particularly mathematics — into career and technical education (CTE) curricula, based on feedback from CTE educators. The research finds that incorporating academic learning into CTE requires substantial time, effort, and resources, and highlights the real implementation challenges educators and curriculum developers must navigate when blending vocational training with academic content.

entries.title: The NIH Undergraduate Scholarship Program: Career Outcomes of Scholars and Non-Awarded Finalists
entries.desc: This evaluation compared career outcomes between NIH Undergraduate Scholarship Program recipients and finalist applicants who did not receive awards, examining whether the program effectively supports disadvantaged undergraduate science students in pursuing biomedical research careers. Scholars were achieving career milestones at high rates and making steady progress toward becoming biomedical researchers, substantially outpacing non-awarded finalists, suggesting the program is operating as intended.

entries.title: Disproportionality in School Discipline: An Assessment in Maryland through 2018
entries.desc: This Regional Educational Laboratory Mid-Atlantic study analyzed disciplinary removal trends in Maryland schools from 2009/10 to 2017/18 using administrative data. Although overall exclusionary discipline rates declined, Black students and students with disabilities continued to be suspended and expelled at more than twice the rates of other students. Even for identical infractions, these groups were significantly more likely to receive out-of-school suspensions, suggesting implicit bias as a contributing factor.

entries.title: Preparing for Life After High School: The Characteristics and Experiences of Youth in Special Education
entries.desc: This NCEE evaluation brief summarizes findings from the National Longitudinal Transition Study 2012, which surveyed secondary school youth with disabilities and their parents to assess IDEA implementation. Youth with disabilities were found to be more socioeconomically disadvantaged, to demonstrate lower academic performance, and to be less likely to take steps toward postsecondary education and employment, with youth with intellectual disabilities, autism, and multiple disabilities facing the highest risks.

entries.title: Building Teacher Capacity to Support English Language Learners in Schools Receiving School Improvement Grants
entries.desc: This NCEE evaluation brief examined staffing and professional development strategies for serving English Language Learners in 11 high-ELL schools receiving federal School Improvement Grant funding (2010–13). Few schools leveraged staffing strategies or hired with ELL expertise in mind, while teachers widely participated in ELL-related PD but received limited hours. Schools that devoted a greater share of PD to ELL topics had teachers more likely to report improved effectiveness.

entries.title: Not Just Numeracy and Literacy: Theory of Mind Development and School Readiness among Low-Income Children
entries.desc: This longitudinal study of 120 low-income preschool and kindergarten children examined how theory of mind development contributes to school readiness, exploring links among theory of mind, understanding of teaching, learning behaviors, and literacy and numeracy skills at school entry. Children's understanding of teaching was particularly important — it predicted changes in literacy scores over time — underscoring that sociocognitive development matters for school readiness beyond traditional academic skills.

entries.title: Student Characteristics and Achievement in 22 KIPP Middle Schools
entries.desc: This multi-year evaluation of the Knowledge Is Power Program (KIPP) employed a matched comparison group design to examine impacts on disadvantaged students across 22 KIPP middle schools. For the vast majority of KIPP schools in the evaluation, impacts on state assessment scores in math and reading were positive, statistically significant, and educationally substantial, providing rigorous evidence for the model's effectiveness.

entries.title: Pre-Kindergarten Impacts Over Time: An Analysis of KIPP Charter Schools
entries.desc: This study tracked outcomes over five years to determine whether benefits from KIPP Pre-K enrollment persisted as students progressed through elementary school. After five years, KIPP Pre-K combined with KIPP early elementary school showed positive and statistically significant impacts on reading and math achievement, with potential advantages for executive function development. Reading skill improvements were sustained over time, though reading comprehension impacts largely dissipated by grade 2.

entries.title: Scaling Programs with Research Evidence and Effectiveness (SPREE)
entries.desc: SPREE uses evaluation and implementation science to determine which programs produce desired outcomes and which organizations are ready for expansion, and can help organizations develop evaluation cultures even when none previously existed.

entries.title: Challenges in Adolescent Reading Intervention: Evidence from a Randomized Control Trial
entries.desc: Reporting on the nation's first RCT of Leveled Literacy Intervention (LLI) in secondary grades in Oakland schools, this study found no measurable improvement in reading comprehension and adverse impacts on ELA/literacy standards mastery. Students pulled from regular classes for LLI were particularly negatively affected, possibly due to missing grade-level content. The research underscores the difficulty of adapting elementary interventions for adolescents without accounting for curriculum continuity.

entries.title: Promise Neighborhoods Case Studies
entries.desc: This case study analysis examined how five Promise Neighborhoods grantees — part of a $100 million federal initiative across 48 lead agencies in 23 states — designed their programs, selected services, and navigated early implementation challenges. Promise Neighborhoods aim to build integrated support systems from early childhood through career preparation in high-poverty communities, offsetting the effects of poverty on children's educational and life outcomes.

entries.title: Missouri's Teacher Career Ladder Program
entries.desc: This working paper analyzed nine years of longitudinal data from 524 Missouri school districts, comparing achievement in districts participating in the state's Career Ladder teacher compensation program against matched non-participating districts. The study found that any positive effect on test scores was likely very small, ranging from negligible to no measurable impact, contributing evidence to debates about differentiated teacher pay structures.

entries.title: Landscape Analysis of Non-Cognitive Measures
entries.desc: This landscape analysis collected and analyzed information about instruments designed to measure learning strategies and student mindsets in middle childhood, examining tools for assessing non-cognitive skills relevant to academic outcomes. The project was part of a broader effort to understand what measures are available, valid, and reliable for assessing the non-academic factors that influence student success.

entries.title: Measuring Charter School Effectiveness Across States
entries.desc: This report outlines methodology developed with New Leaders for identifying effective schools and teachers across a consortium of 99 charter schools in 18 states and Washington, DC. The approach was customized based on each partner's priorities and available performance data, with plans for future refinement and incorporation of additional metrics, offering a model for multi-state charter effectiveness measurement.
